evals.prompt: Apply my style to this document: ~/.agents/skill-workspaces/apply-style-workspace/test-docs/test1-rate-limiting.md
evals.expected_output: A restyled version with callouts, emoji identity for the 3 strategies, comparison table, bullet points instead of prose, no filler phrases, restructured to lead with recommendations
expectations: No filler phrases like 'it is important to note', 'in this document we will', 'in conclusion', 'it is worth mentioning'
Contains at least 2 Obsidian-style callouts using '> [!type]' syntax (e.g., [!important], [!warning], [!tip], [!note])
The three strategies (fixed window, sliding window, token bucket) each have a consistent emoji assigned
Contains at least one markdown comparison table
Callout content uses bullet points, not prose paragraphs (no multi-sentence blocks inside > lines without bullet markers)
Uses bullet points extensively — the majority of explanatory content is bulleted, not in paragraph form
Uses inline code formatting for technical terms like field names, config values, and strategy names
The recommendation (token bucket for public, fixed window for internal) appears in a prominent [!important] callout, not buried in a paragraph

evals.prompt: Apply my style to this document: ~/.agents/skill-workspaces/apply-style-workspace/test-docs/test2-caching-gotchas.md
evals.expected_output: A restyled version where existing callout prose is converted to bullets, the critical write-through+sliding TTL finding becomes a Pattern C warning, hierarchical bullets are used, and edge cases get expressive emoji
expectations: The [!important] callout about TTL modes contains bullet points, not the original prose paragraph
The [!warning] about write-through + sliding TTL uses Pattern C structure: opening statement, detail bullets, bolded action item
Edge cases have expressive emoji (e.g., thundering herd, race condition, partial invalidation each get a distinct emoji)
Uses hierarchical (nested) bullet points — at least 3 instances of indented sub-bullets
No filler phrases like 'it is important to note', 'in conclusion', 'it is worth mentioning'
Contains at least 3 Obsidian-style callouts using '> [!type]' syntax
The three nested-object approaches (eager invalidation, version tags, short TTL) are presented in a structured format, not numbered prose paragraphs
Reference to docs/cache-benchmarks.md uses Tier 2 style (italic footnote below horizontal rule), not an inline link

evals.prompt: Apply my style to this document: ~/.agents/skill-workspaces/apply-style-workspace/test-docs/test3-deploy-modes.md
evals.expected_output: A restyled version with emoji identity for the 3 deploy modes, restructured to lead with the recommendation, comparison table, callouts for key rules and the March 12th warning, no trailing conclusion
expectations: The three deploy modes (blue-green, rolling, canary) each have a consistent emoji assigned that appears in tables and bullet lists
Contains a comparison/summary table for the deploy modes
The 'never use rolling deploy for shared state' warning appears in a [!warning] callout, not a paragraph
No trailing conclusion paragraph — the document does not end with 'in conclusion' or a summary restating what was already said
No filler phrases like 'it is important to note', 'in this section we will', 'in conclusion', 'it is worth mentioning'
The recommendation section uses an [!important] callout with bullet points mapping scenarios to modes
Uses bullet points extensively — deploy mode descriptions are bulleted, not multi-paragraph prose
Reference to docs/deploy-migration-runbook.md uses one of the two-tier reference styles (either [!tip] callout or italic footnote)

evals.prompt: Apply my style to this document: ~/.agents/skill-workspaces/apply-style-workspace/test-docs/test4-repetition-behavior.md
evals.expected_output: A restyled version of a real empirical reference doc — already partially structured, but needs emoji identity for schedule types, callouts for key rules and warnings, tighter prose, and better use of the style's structural patterns
expectations: The three schedule types use consistent emoji identity throughout: catch-up gets a positive/recommended emoji, regularly gets a neutral emoji, from-completion gets a caution emoji
The fundamental asymmetry rule (Part 3) appears in an [!important] callout, not just as a plain table under a heading
The 14-day divergence finding (Part 5) appears in a [!warning] callout — this is a dramatic gotcha that deserves Pattern C treatment
The MCP Server Warning section (Part 7) uses a [!warning] callout for the incorrect warning text
The decision guide (Part 8) uses bullet points with emoji identity matching the schedule types
No multi-sentence prose paragraphs used where bullets would work — intro paragraphs are 1-2 sentences max before switching to structured content
Technical terms like schedule type names, field names, and config values use inline code formatting
The methodology section is de-emphasized relative to the findings — either moved to a secondary reference position or clearly separated as supplementary material
All existing tables are preserved or improved — no tables removed or converted to less scannable formats
All technical facts and empirical findings are preserved — no content lost, no claims changed or softened
